title: Clinical effectiveness of manual therapy for the management of musculoskeletal and non-musculoskeletal conditions: systematic review and update of UK evidence report
research_question: To update and extend the UK evidence report on the clinical effectiveness of manual therapy for musculoskeletal and non-musculoskeletal conditions, focusing on areas previously rated as inconclusive or negative or not covered.
methods_summary: Systematic review with literature search of over 10 databases (August 2011, updated March 2013).
Inclusion of systematic reviews, RCTs, controlled clinical trials, cohort studies, and qualitative studies.
Quality assessment using AMSTAR, Cochrane Risk of Bias, CRD checklist, and CASP.
Evidence rating based on consistency, study size, and quality (high, moderate, inconclusive).
Comparison of new evidence against ratings from the Bronfort et al. UK evidence report.
dataset: 25,539 records identified; 178 new studies included (72 systematic reviews, 96 RCTs, 10 non-randomized primary studies).
key_findings: Most inconclusive or moderate evidence ratings from the UK evidence report were confirmed.

Evidence ratings changed from inconclusive to moderate in only three cases: manipulation/mobilisation with exercise for rotator cuff disorder, spinal mobilisation for cervicogenic headache, and mobilisation for miscellaneous headache.
Evidence for non-musculoskeletal conditions was mostly rated as inconclusive.
Overall, limited high-quality evidence for manual therapy effectiveness; most evidence was low to moderate quality and inconsistent.
extracted_insights: The review confirms that manual therapy evidence remains largely inconclusive for many conditions, with only a few upgrades from inconclusive to moderate. High-quality evidence is scarce, and methodological diversity limits conclusions. Further research is needed, particularly for non-musculoskeletal conditions.
